# Raw research: AI and theological anthropology

**Prompt:** [docs/build/research/prompts/08-ai-theological-anthropology.md](../prompts/08-ai-theological-anthropology.md)  
**Date:** 2026-04-12  
**Methods:** OpenAlex API, web search, direct fetch (WCC statement, Rome Call site, internal repo grep). **Alan Hirsch corpus:** canonical path from `article-corpus` skill (`alan-books` repo) not mounted here; internal book trajectory / messaging used as substitute pointers.

---

## 1. Annotated bibliography — major theological voices on AI

| Voice | Role / tradition | Representative work(s) | Notes for Movemental |
|-------|------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Noreen L. Herzfeld** | Lutheran, computer scientist | *In Our Image: Artificial Intelligence and the Human Spirit* (Fortress, 2002) — ~79 citations in OpenAlex index | Earliest book-length Christian theological treatment widely cited; **imago Dei**, relational anthropology, typology of AI schools vs. theological anthropologies (Niebuhr, von Rad, Barth). |
| **Brent Waters** | Protestant social ethics | *From Human to Posthuman* (Routledge, 2006); later essays on technology and “remaining creaturely” | Frames technology as **cultural faith**; anticipates transhumanist pressure; not AI-specific but foundational for **creaturely limits**. |
| **Albert Borgmann** | Lutheran philosopher of technology | *Technology and the Character of Contemporary Life* (1984); *Power Failure* | **Device paradigm** vs. **focal practices** — secular-academic vocabulary that many Christian ethicists adopt; supports “what AI displaces” without using Christian terms. |
| **James K.A. Smith** | Reformed phenomenology of culture | *Desiring the Kingdom* / *Imagining the Kingdom* / *You Are What You Love* | **Embodied habituation**, liturgical formation, cultural liturgies — primary academic anchor for “formation isn’t information”; not about AI per se but heavily cited in **digital worship / online church** debates. |
| **Sherry Turkle** | Social studies of science / MIT | *Alone Together* (2011) etc. | Secular but ubiquitous in pastoral AI talks: **relational substitution**, **authenticity**; useful evidence layer, not a theology of sacrament. |
| **Jason Thacker** | Evangelical (ERLC) | *The Age of AI* (Zondervan, 2020); ERLC AI initiatives | **Imago Dei** vs. machine anthropology; “tool not savior”; bridges policy and congregational discourse. |
| **Ted Peters** | Lutheran systematic / religion-and-science | *Theology and Science* editorship; *AI and IA: Utopia or Extinction?* (ed.); extensive public theology on AI, virtue, holiness | Futurist eschatology + science dialogue; asks whether AI offers **shortcuts to virtue** (critique parallel to “formation cannot be automated”). |
| **Catholic magisterium-adjacent** | Pontifical Academy for Life, dicasteries | **Rome Call for AI Ethics** (28 Feb 2020); ongoing “RenAIssance” / algorethics agenda | Principles: transparency, inclusion, responsibility, impartiality, reliability, security/privacy. Signed by Microsoft, IBM, FAO, Italian government initially; later **multi-religious** expansions (e.g. Hiroshima 2024). Official site: https://www.romecall.org/ |
| **Ecumenical** | WCC | “New and emerging technologies…” (Executive Committee, Nov 2022); **“Statement on the Unregulated Development of Artificial Intelligence”** (Central Committee, June 2023) | Explicitly ties AI to **anthropology / imago Dei** (footnotes reference Faith & Order study doc); names **idolatry**, regulation, digital divide, ministry risks (plagiarism, “digital facsimiles”). URL: https://www.oikoumene.org/resources/documents/statement-on-the-unregulated-development-of-artificial-intelligence |
| **Erin Green** (example in WCC footnote) | Ecotheology | “Sallie McFague and an Ecotheological Response to Artificial Intelligence,” *Ecumenical Review* 72:2 (2020) | Illustrates **ecological / contextual** theologies entering AI discourse. |

### OpenAlex snapshot — query `theological anthropology artificial intelligence` (2026-04-12)

High-level hit count **~6,322** works (noisy). Top of first page included:

- *Theological Anthropology Progressing through Artificial Intelligence* (2024) — DOI `10.4324/9781032646732-15`
- *Artificial Intelligence and Christian Ethics: A Perspective from Theological Anthropology* (2019) — `10.18804/jyt.2019.06.48`
- *Strong Artificial Intelligence and Theological Anthropology: One Problem, Two Solutions* (2022) — Springer chapter DOI truncated in API output
- Herzfeld *In Our Image* (2002) — **79** cited_by_count in index
- *Human-Level, but Non-Humanlike* (2021) — **33** cites — `10.1628/ptsc-2021-0006`

**Screening note:** Many hits are **2023–2025** explosion of conference papers and chapters with **1–2 citations** — field is **young and fragmented**.

| Body | Document | Date | URL / locator |
|------|----------|------|----------------|
| **Southern Baptist Convention** | Resolution “On Artificial Intelligence and Emerging Technologies” | Adopted **2023** (New Orleans annual meeting) | https://www.sbc.net/resource-library/resolutions/on-artificial-intelligence-and-emerging-technologies/ |
| **ERLC / Evangelical** | “Evangelical Statement of Principles on Artificial Intelligence” | **2019** (precursor to SBC resolution; 70+ signers) | Covered in Baptist Press / Thacker’s commentary |

| **WCC** | Guidelines for Use of Generative AI | **2025** (per search snippet) | https://oikoumene.org/resources/documents/guidelines-for-the-use-of-generative-ai |
| **Roman Catholic** | Rome Call for AI Ethics | **28 Feb 2020** | https://www.romecall.org/ |
| **Church of England** | EIAG AI advice (ethical investment / AI typology); Synod path | **Dec 2024** PDF noted in search; **Rome Call** signed by Archbishop of Canterbury **2024** | https://www.churchofengland.org/ (resource PDF path per search: `eiag-artificial-intelligence-advice-2024.pdf`) |
| **PC(USA)** | General Assembly overture / “Faith and AI” / Office of Innovation “AI & the Church” | **2024–2026** | https://www.pcusa.org/ — multiple agency pages; **DOE-07** study team (2024 GA) |
| **PCA** | No single “AI resolution” surfaced in quick search; **commentary** (e.g. Jude 3 / pastoral soul-care warnings) | Ongoing | Treat as **decentralized** unless a GA resolution is later located |

**Gap:** Methodist (UMC / Global Methodist), Anglican Communion **global** instruments beyond C of E, Canadian / Australian bishops’ conferences — **not** fully inventoried in this pass.

---

## 3. Embodiment theology & digital ministry

### 3.1 Theological sources (conceptual)

- **Sacramental theology:** Eucharistic presence debates are the **strictest** articulation of why “digital” may or may not carry **the same kind** of presence as embodied assembly.
- **Incarnational mission (missiology):** “Word became flesh” as methodological priority — supports **digital as auxiliary**, not substitute, when stated carefully.
- **James K.A. Smith:** habit, desire, body — **formation** requires **repeated embodied practice** in community; screens compete with **liturgies** of consumer tech.

### 3.2 Empirical / pastoral literature (COVID)

OpenAlex sample query `online church worship embodied COVID` (top results by relevance):

- *The Absence of Presence and the Presence of Absence: Social Distancing, Sacraments, and…* (2020) — **95** citations — directly addresses **sacramental absence** under distancing.
- *The Distanced Church: Reflections on Doing Church Online* (2020) — **57** citations.

**Interpretation:** COVID did not “settle” the question; it **forced** provisional practices and produced a **large** reflective literature split between **expansionists** (“hybrid is real church”) and **sacramental realists** (“emergency ≠ norm”).

### 3.3 Neuroscience / 4E cognition

- **4E cognition** (embodied, embedded, extended, enactive) is often used in philosophy of mind to **blur** brain/body/world boundaries — can be cited **either** to support “online is still embodied because humans are always hybrid with tools” **or** to warn that **outsourcing cognitive effort** reshapes skill and character. Movemental should pick one coherent use.

---

## 4. “Refusal as love” & sacred limits

### 4.1 In Christian AI ethics literature

- **Explicit phrase** “refusal as love” tied to AI is **not** a standard indexed keyword in OpenAlex hits from this session.
- **Adjacent established moves:**
  - **Sabbath / rest** theologies (Jewish and Christian) — principled **non-use** of productive technology in time.
  - **Wendell Berry**-style agrarian **refusal** of “inevitable” tech — widely invoked in Christian tech ethics blogosphere (e.g. AI and Faith pieces on Berry + COVID + AI).
  - **Virtue ethics** questions (Peters and others): Is AI a **shortcut to holiness**? That is structurally similar to “refusal protects sacred process” without the romantic wording.

### 4.2 Secular AI ethics “red lines”

- EU AI Act risk tiers; military autonomous weapons bans; workplace surveillance limits — **harm-avoidance** framing dominates.
- **Religious difference:** sacred refusal is often **teleological** (protects **formation**, **worship**, **truth-telling as witness**) rather than only **risk minimization**.

### 4.3 Interfaith parallels (high level)

- **Jewish law:** instruments on Shabbat; emerging responsa on generative tools — boundary logic.
- **Islam:** classical **image-making** debates; contemporary **fiqh** on AI tools varies by school.
- **Buddhist** contexts: attention economy critiques; **non-harm** applied to deception by chatbots.

Movemental can cite these **carefully** as **analogies**, not as deep comparative-religion claims without specialist review.

---

## 5. “Furnace and hearth” metaphor — genealogy

### 5.1 Movemental corpus (this repo)

- **Copy deck:** `docs/articles/01_copy-deck-why-movemental.md` — §7 Alternative A: **“AI is a furnace. It needs a hearth.”** Body: AI drafts/summarizes/scales but “cannot bear witness… cannot stand in fellowship…”
- **Messaging:** `docs/arguments/custom-gpt/messaging-07-business-audience-ai.md` and `messaging-08-by-page.md` echo the headline.

### 5.2 Prior art (partial)

- **Industrial / domestic history:** scholarship on shift from **hearth** (center of skill, warmth, gathering) to **basement furnace** (black-box comfort) — culturally parallel to **device paradigm** (Borgmann) and Andy Crouch-style **culture making** essays (e.g. “Rekindling Old Fires” on hearth imagery — verify exact title before citing as peer source).
- **Biblical resonance:** **furnace** as trial/refinement (Daniel 3, etc.) — could be invoked **accidentally** if readers hear “judgment” not “industrial heat.” Worth A/B testing metaphor with theologians.

**Assessment:** The **pairing** “furnace + hearth” as a **tagline for generative AI** appears **Movemental-specific** in public copy found here; **components** (hearth as domestic center; furnace as industrial power) draw on **long-standing cultural tropes** in philosophy of technology and American domestic history.

---

## 6. Movemental argument index — mapping to literature

| ID (from prompt) | Claim (short) | Established in literature? | Originality |
|------------------|---------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| book-003 | AI as anthropological problem | Common in Thacker, WCC, Catholic social thought, Herzfeld | **Framing** is mainstream **now**; was **prophetic** in Herzfeld 2002 |
| C-AU01 | Furnace needs hearth; not savior/threat | “Tool not savior” = Thacker; furnace/hearth = **Movemental packaging** | **Metaphor** distinctive; **substance** overlaps evangelical + Borgmann |
| book-045 | Vocation in AI age | Large humanities + Christian ethics overlap | Needs **specific** vocational theology (Luther / Catholic CST) citations to lift above generic |
| C-T01 | Embodied ministry not automatable | Strong in sacramental + Smith streams; contested in hybrid-church literature | **Consensus among sacramental realists**; not universal |
| C-A05 | Refusal as love / sacred protection | Partial parallels (Sabbath, virtue, Berry); phrase **not** standard | **Rhetorical innovation** unless sourced to a named author |
| business-055 | What AI displaces (relationship, prayer, presence…) | Turkle + Borgmann + pastoral burnout literature | **List** is pedagogically useful; not novel as sociology |
| C-T03 | Christocentric spine load-bearing | Standard confessional claim | **Differentiation vs. secular** platforms is real if consistently executed |
| business-036 | Communitas, risk, mission | Turner / Hirsch corpus theme | **Hirsch network** is Movemental’s natural **primary** source for this strand |

## 9. Assessment: original vs. established

**Established:** imago Dei dignity; AI as moral / anthropological crisis; regulation + corporate power critique (WCC); Catholic principles-based ethics (Rome Call); formation > information (Smith); embodiment debates (COVID lit); tool vs. idol framing.

**Movemental-inflected:** explicit **movement leader** vocational frame + **credibility / scenius** stack (adjacent research series); **furnace/hearth** metaphor; tight coupling of AI posture to **communitas** and **Christocentric** public theology **in a platform narrative**.

**Risk:** If theological language functions only as **marketing differentiation**, secular readers will treat it as **branding**; academic theologians will ask for **footnotes to Aquinas, Barth, Tanner, CST paragraphs**. The fix is **citation thickness** and **transparent limits** on what the product can do for souls.
